title: The Top Five Movies of 2020
format: Sean Fennessey, Amanda Dobbins, Chris Ryan, and Adam Nayman each rank their five best films of a locked-down, streaming-heavy year. Three of the four land on the same movie at the very top.

films.title: First Cow
films.note: No. 1 - Kelly Reichardt's frontier-friendship film, which premiered at NYFF in 2019 and finally got released this year. Adam calls Reichardt "the great filmmaker of American failure" and says it's the most emotionally engaging thing he saw all year.

films.title: Mangrove
films.note: No. 2 - Steve McQueen's Small Axe courtroom drama about the Mangrove Nine. Adam has mixed feelings about McQueen generally but singles out a wordless moment - a man pulling his daughter out of a protest march - as the most powerful thing he saw in a movie this year.

films.title: Vitalina Varela
films.note: No. 3 - Pedro Costa's Portuguese drama about a woman arriving in Lisbon just after her husband's death. Adam calls it the most beautiful piece of filmmaking he saw this year and recommends watching it in a completely dark room.

films.title: An Evening with Tim Heidecker
films.note: No. 4 - Ben Berman's anti-comedy special, with Heidecker playing a version of himself performing deliberately bad standup. Adam calls it misanthropic, cathartic, and brilliant.

films.title: Time
films.note: No. 5 - Garrett Bradley's documentary about a woman's 20-year fight to free her incarcerated husband, built around her own home-video footage - added in after the film was already cut.

films.title: The Vast of Night
films.note: No. 1 - Andrew Patterson's $700,000 UFO thriller set in 1950s New Mexico. Chris calls it the best time he had watching a movie all year and says he restarted it the moment it ended.

films.title: Mank
films.note: No. 2 - one of three picks (with Amanda and Sean) that converge on the same film, saved for the episode's finale.

films.title: The Nest
films.note: No. 3 - Sean Durkin's marriage-collapse drama with Jude Law and Carrie Coon, set in the mid-'80s. Chris calls Coon's performance his favorite of the year.

films.title: Shithouse
films.note: No. 4 - Cooper Raiff's debut about a homesick college freshman, which Chris says knocked him back with how emotionally upfront it is.

films.title: Host
films.note: No. 5 - Rob Savage's 56-minute Zoom-seance horror movie on Shudder. Chris calls it the movie he'll most associate with 2020.

films.title: Never Rarely Sometimes Always
films.note: No. 1 - Eliza Hittman's film about a Pennsylvania teenager traveling to New York for an abortion. Amanda calls it the movie that felt most set in the real world this year.

films.title: Time
films.note: No. 2 - Garrett Bradley's documentary, also on Adam's list. Amanda watched it alone in her living room and found the experience of watching someone's private footage in her own personal space especially moving.

films.title: Mank
films.note: No. 3 - saved for the finale; the third of three picks (with Chris and Sean) that all land on the same film.

films.title: David Byrne's American Utopia
films.note: No. 4 - Spike Lee's filmed version of Byrne's Broadway show. Amanda calls it her most rapturous and emotional movie-watching experience of the year.

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: No. 5 (tied) - one of two rom-coms Amanda picked after being encouraged to be idiosyncratic; both were meant for theaters and went straight to Hulu instead.

films.title: Happiest Season
films.note: No. 5 (tied) - the other Hulu rom-com pick, Amanda's platform being "make rom-coms good and quality again."

films.title: Mank
films.note: No. 1 - David Fincher's black-and-white portrait of screenwriter Herman Mankiewicz, the subject of the episode's closing discourse debate. Sean, Chris, and Amanda all put it in their top five.

films.title: Mangrove
films.note: No. 2 (tied) - unlike Adam, who picked only one Small Axe film, Sean picked both Mangrove and Lover's Rock.

films.title: Lover's Rock
films.note: No. 2 (tied) - the other Small Axe pick, an all-night house-party film Sean says he's never seen anything quite like, built around one long dance-floor sequence.

films.title: Boys State
films.note: No. 3 - a documentary about a mock-government summer camp for teenage boys in Texas, directed by Jesse Moss and Amanda McBaine.

films.title: Soul
films.note: No. 4 - Pixar's Pete Docter film about where souls come from, premiering on Disney+ on Christmas Day. Sean calls it one of the bleakest existential explorations he's seen in a long time.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: No. 5 - Chloe Zhao's film starring Frances McDormand, which Sean saw with Amanda at a drive-in screening - one of the only theatrical experiences either had after February.

films: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
